channel.title: Thoughts, tutorials, whatever I feel like writing on d3adb5's personal website
channel.description: Recent content in Thoughts, tutorials, whatever I feel like writing on d3adb5's personal website

item.title: Using Git outside of programming
item.description: It is public knowledge that versioning is important when writing documents, be it source code for a project written in a programming language, drafts of a book, or meeting minutes. GitHub, for instance, uses Git for their site policy, documentation, and roadmap, none of which involve or focus on programming. But what if we don’t care too much about versioning itself? Keeping a history of versions of a piece of work doesn’t come to mind so naturally, especially for those of us who don’t want to show whatever we’re working on to anybody until we’re perfectly happy with it.

item.title: On linear history with Git: rebase, merge, squash
item.description: DISCLAIMER: I do not claim to be an expert on Git, nor do I claim to have a lot of experience in source code management tools. This article is being written as I decide what I want for my own projects, and how I position myself on the application of each “philosophy”, so to speak. This topic is controversial in ways I hadn’t even imagined before. Note: Decent knowledge of Git and its terminology is assumed here.

item.title: Experiences with CI/CD and DevOps
item.description: WARNING: This became such a lengthy article despite the relative lack of detail that it forced me to include a table of contents. Hopefully it isn’t too tiresome to read through, and whoever reads it can jump to whatever topic might interest them. The goal of this blogpost is to give the reader an overview of what it was like for me to get accustomed to and implement some continuous integration (CI) and continuous deployment (CD) solutions to my own personal projects.
